text: In just five years, a startup born from a split at OpenAI became one of the most valuable companies on Earth, worth nearly one trillion dollars. This is the story of Anthropic and their incredible rise.
visual: intro montage with company logo

text: To understand Anthropic, we go back to twenty twenty-one. Tensions at OpenAI were boiling over. Dario Amodei, the VP of Research, and his sister Daniela, who led safety and policy, believed AI safety was being pushed aside in the race to commercialize.
visual: openai logo with tension indicators

text: So in January twenty twenty-one, Dario, Daniela, and seven other researchers walked out and founded Anthropic as a public benefit corporation. Their mission was radical: build incredibly powerful AI, but make safety the priority, not an afterthought.
visual: people icons streaming out of one building into new one

text: The name Anthropic comes from the anthropic principle in physics, the idea that the universe's laws seem fine-tuned for intelligent life. They believed intelligence itself needed careful stewardship.
visual: cosmic imagery with name reveal animation

text: In May twenty twenty-one, just months after founding, Anthropic raised one hundred twenty-four million dollars from heavyweights like Skype co-founder Jaan Tallinn, Facebook co-founder Dustin Moskovitz, and former Google CEO Eric Schmidt.
visual: investor logos cascading in

text: They built in stealth for nearly two years, creating Claude, named after Claude Shannon, the father of information theory. In twenty twenty-two, they raised a five hundred eighty million dollar Series B. The lead investor was FTX, run by Sam Bankman-Fried. Within months, FTX collapsed in one of the biggest frauds in history.
visual: Claude Shannon photo, FTX logo crumbling into pieces

text: Anthropic had to buy back FTX's stake to free itself from the association. It was a near-death experience for the young company, but they survived and kept building.
visual: company rising from wreckage animation

text: They pioneered a breakthrough approach called Constitutional AI. Instead of having humans rate millions of responses, they gave the model a written constitution to judge itself against. The model would generate a response, critique its own output, and revise it to be more helpful, honest, and harmless.
visual: constitution document, self-critique loop diagram with arrows

text: Drawing inspiration from the UN Declaration of Human Rights and Apple's Terms of Service, it was the first time an AI company published the actual principles guiding their model's behavior.
visual: source documents shown with highlight animation

text: In March twenty twenty-three, Claude one finally launched with a one hundred thousand token context window and the core H-H-H principles: helpful, harmless, and honest. That same year, Amazon invested one point three billion dollars, and Google committed up to two billion. Big Tech was placing their bets on safety-first AI.
visual: Claude 1 launch screen, Amazon and Google logos with dollar signs

text: Claude two launched in July twenty twenty-three, doubling down on reasoning and coding. Then Claude two point one introduced tool use support and significantly reduced hallucinations.
visual: Claude 2 launch with feature comparison

text: The real breakthrough came March fourth, twenty twenty-four. Anthropic unveiled the Claude three family: three models in one launch. Haiku for speed, Sonnet for balance, and Opus for maximum power. Opus outperformed GPT-4 on nearly every benchmark from math to coding to image analysis.
visual: three tier cards appearing with spring animation, benchmark chart

text: The three-tier system was genius. Customers could pick the right model for each job: Haiku for real-time chat, Sonnet for daily work, and Opus for the toughest problems. It democratized access to frontier AI.
visual: tier diagram with use case labels

text: In June twenty twenty-four, Claude three point five Sonnet shocked the industry by delivering Opus-level performance at a fraction of the cost. It became the best coding model available. Developers flocked to it. By end of twenty twenty-four, revenue had exploded from eighty-seven million to over one billion dollars.
visual: Sonnet matching Opus performance chart, revenue graph soaring

text: Amazon deepened its commitment, bringing their total investment to eight billion dollars. Anthropic was no longer a startup. It was a powerhouse.
visual: Amazon logo with 8 billion dollar badge

text: February twenty twenty-five brought Claude three point seven Sonnet with extended thinking, showing its chain-of-thought reasoning before answering for the first time. Then in May, the Claude four generation launched with massive coding gains and Claude Code, an AI coding agent that became a revenue juggernaut.
visual: thinking visualization, Claude 4 and Claude Code badges

text: By September, Anthropic had raised thirteen billion dollars in Series F at a one hundred eighty-three billion dollar valuation. The company hit a fourteen billion dollar revenue run rate by February twenty twenty-six.
visual: funding milestones infographic

text: February twenty twenty-six was a landmark month. Anthropic raised thirty billion dollars in Series G at a three hundred eighty billion dollar valuation. It was the second largest private fundraising round in history.
visual: money flowing into company animation

text: By April, annualized revenue had exploded to thirty billion dollars. Eighty times growth in just over a year. Over three hundred thousand business customers, including eight of the Fortune ten companies.
visual: revenue explosion chart, customer logos array

text: Claude Opus four point seven in April improved coding, vision, and complex multi-step reasoning. Claude Design let users create prototypes just by describing them. Then Opus four point eight in May introduced effort control, a dial that scales how deeply the model thinks, plus Dynamic Workflows that spawn up to a thousand parallel sub-agents.
visual: Claude Design demo interface, effort dial, agent tree diagram

text: Then came June ninth, twenty twenty-six. Claude Fable five launched, Anthropic's first Mythos-class model, a tier above Opus. It scored over eighty percent on SWE-bench Pro, eleven points ahead of the next best. Stripe used it to migrate a fifty-million-line codebase in a single day, a task that took a team over two months.
visual: Fable 5 crown reveal, Stripe logo, code migration visualization

text: But just three days later, on June twelfth, the US government issued an export control directive citing national security concerns over a potential jailbreak. Anthropic was forced to disable Fable five for all customers worldwide. It was the first time a government had pulled an AI model from the market. Anthropic publicly disagreed but is working to restore access.
visual: government document, shutdown notice animation

text: On June first, twenty twenty-six, Anthropic filed confidentially for an IPO at a nine hundred sixty-five billion dollar valuation, after closing a sixty-five billion dollar Series H round. From zero to nearly one trillion in five years.
visual: IPO document, valuation milestone chart

text: Anthropic now has over twenty-five hundred employees, up from two hundred forty just three years ago. They have raised over one hundred billion dollars in total funding. Annualized revenue has hit forty-seven billion dollars.
visual: employee growth chart, funding total graphic

text: What makes Anthropic different is their philosophy. They are a public benefit corporation, legally required to consider the public interest. They publish their constitution. They prioritize interpretability, trying to understand how these models actually think.
visual: philosophy pillars with icons

text: But challenges remain. Critics point to the concentration of power. Anthropic is backed by Amazon, Google, Microsoft, and Nvidia. When the same models run the world's infrastructure, who holds them accountable?
visual: power concentration web graphic

text: Yet there is no denying the impact. From a splinter group of nine people with a dream to a near trillion dollar company building what may be the most important technology of our lifetime. Dario and Daniela Amodei took a bet that safety and scale could go hand in hand. So far, they are winning.
visual: journey from 9 people to global scale animation

text: The story of Anthropic is still being written. With Fable five's suspension, the upcoming IPO, and the race against OpenAI, one thing is clear: the future of intelligence is being shaped right now, and it might just be in safe hands.
visual: closing shot with logo, tagline, and call to action
